    Sarah Swiston

    About

    I am currently a postdoctoral teaching fellow at Morningside University. My work centers on teaching project-based courses for first-year STEM students and studying the impact of these courses on student agency, scientific identity, and scientific reasoning. I am also involved in continuing biological research focused on developing mathematical models to investigate how species evolve over time and move through space.

    Previously, I completed my Ph.D. in Ecology & Evolutionary Biology at Washington University in Saint Louis, in the lab of Michael Landis. My dissertation focused on phylogenetic methods for historical biogeography, investigating the relationships between evolving lineages and their environments and developing models that incorporate paleogeographic and paleoenvironmental information alongside phylogenetic and species range data. I have done work on both lizards and plants, but herpetology will always have my heart.

    Methods development also involves the creation of user-friendly software, tutorials, and workshops. To make the phylogenetic software required to run our models more approachable, I created a containerized set of programs using Docker. The PhyloDocker image allows users to bypass the lengthy installation process and version compatibility problems. I also participated in the creation of a series of RevBayes tutorials and teaching materials for the Phylogenetic Biogeography Workshop, which I co-instructed at Washington University in Saint Louis (June 2024) and at Evolution (June 2025).

    I am also committed to teaching and pedagogical research. I obtained a Teaching Citation and completed the Professional Development in Teaching program through the Washington University Center for Teaching and Learning (CTL), which involved several teaching and mentorship experiences, workshops, and the implementation of an education research project. My project centered on assessing the efficacy of the Phylogenetic Biogeography Workshop and the activities we incorporated. I was also a graduate student fellow at the CTL, working on developing and implementing programming for other students.

    Using the course design strategies I learned through my work with the CTL, I developed a graduate-level course called Mathematical Modeling in Ecology and Evolution, which I taught Spring 2026 as an instructor of record.

    This fall, I will be teaching two courses for first-year STEM students at Morningside: Critical Inquiry and Communication, and NextGen STEM (a project-based course). My career goals are to teach biology at a primarily undergraduate institution, to mentor undergraduate researchers, and to continue engaging in pedagogical research.

    A full CV can be found here: CV
